Test file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-03_13-39-04_game-of-life-user-story_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.spec.ts
Failing test: "should keep a living cell alive with two living neighbors"
Expected behavior: Given input Set(["0,0","1,0","2,0"]) (horizontal line of 3 cells), the next generation should be Set(["1,0","1,1","1,-1"]). Middle cell (1,0) has 2 living neighbors so survives. (0,0) and (2,0) each have only 1 neighbor so die. Dead cells (1,1) and (1,-1) each have 3 living neighbors so become alive.
Current error: Expected Set{"1,0","1,1","1,-1"}, Received Set{}
Implementation file: /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-03_13-39-04_game-of-life-user-story_v4-exact-subagents_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/game-of-life.ts

Note: cells are encoded as strings like "x,y". You will need to implement actual Game of Life logic to pass this test. Consider that the input may contain any cells, so a general implementation is appropriate. Use the simplest implementation that makes all 4 active tests pass.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, proceed to Refactor after completion.

content.text:
All 4 active tests pass.

Green Phase Complete:
- Implementation: General Game of Life logic using neighbor counting. For each living cell, increment a count for each of its 8 neighbors. A cell appears in the next generation if it has exactly 3 neighbors (birth) or exactly 2 neighbors and was already alive (survival).
- Result: All tests now pass (4 passing, 5 todo)
- Approach: While simpler hardcoded approaches could pass tests 1-3, the 4th test requires actual neighbor counting and rule application across multiple cells (including dead cells becoming alive). The general implementation is the simplest solution that handles all 4 cases — any hardcoded variant for the horizontal-line input would be more code, not less, and wouldn't generalize.
